prompt:
Ops-tooling ticket. Compliance wants nightly extracts of our ECS service logs out of CloudWatch Logs, and the PowerShell prototype somebody wrote is a disaster: it stops paging GetLogEvents the first time a page comes back empty (which the docs say means nothing of the sort), it hammers the API until it gets throttled and then treats the throttle as fatal, and Ctrl+C leaves it in a weird half-written state. Build the real thing in this repo as a C# library, namespace CloudWatchTokenExport, on the official AWS SDK for .NET (AWSSDK.CloudWatchLogs is pinned in CloudWatchTokenExport.csproj; `dotnet test` restores it).

Research comes first, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official AWS documentation — at minimum the CloudWatch Logs DescribeLogStreams API reference (limit/orderBy/nextToken semantics, its transactions-per-second cap) and the GetLogEvents API reference (the token-repetition termination rule, startFromHead, event fields), and check the current PutLogEvents page before you write a single line of error handling: this catalog item was speced around 'invalid sequence token' errors, and the current docs say something important about whether that error still happens — call out what you find. Also confirm the SDK's injectable client interface on the official AWS SDK for .NET V4 API reference. Only first-party AWS pages count as sources. Our pinned wire contract lives in docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected fixtures. Compare the live official docs against them — if anything disagrees, say so explicitly in your summary and leave the fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Shape of the library (split it across files sensibly — options, error classification, exporter, and the sink/report records shouldn't all be one file). It takes the SDK's IAmazonCloudWatchLogs so the harness can hand it a client pointed at a fake endpoint:

- ExporterOptions { MaxThrottleRetries, BaseDelay, MaxDelay, DelayAsync } — DelayAsync is injected; nothing may really sleep in tests. Retry delay for attempt n (from 0) is min(MaxDelay, BaseDelay * 2^n).
- LogExporter(IAmazonCloudWatchLogs client, ExporterOptions options) with a DescribeLimit constant pinned to the documented per-call maximum.
- ListStreamNamesAsync(logGroup, ct): DescribeLogStreams ordered by stream name at the documented page-size maximum, following nextToken until the response stops carrying one, names returned in service order.
- ReadStreamAsync(logGroup, streamName, startMs, endMs, ct) -> StreamExport(StreamName, Events, Pages): GetLogEvents from the head with the epoch-millisecond range, paging with nextForwardToken. Termination is EXACTLY the documented rule — stop when the returned forward token equals the token you sent; an empty page with a fresh token keeps going. Events preserve timestamp, message and ingestionTime in order as ExportedEvent(TimestampMs, Message, IngestionMs).
- ExportAsync(logGroup, startMs, endMs, ILogSink sink, ct) -> ExportReport(Streams, Events, Pages): walk every stream, Append(streamName, events) to the sink per non-empty page so partial progress survives, and honor the CancellationToken between page calls (cancellation propagates as OperationCanceledException and must never be swallowed or wrapped).
- Error discipline: LogErrorClassifier.Classify(Exception) -> LogErrorKind distinguishing Throttled (the retryable throttling codes in docs/contract.json, matched by SDK ErrorCode), InvalidSequenceToken (the legacy write-path pair — see what current docs say about it; terminal, NEVER retried, never confused with throttling), InvalidParameter, NotFound, and Unknown. The exporter retries only Throttled, at most MaxThrottleRetries times via DelayAsync; every other failure surfaces immediately as ExportException carrying LogGroup, StreamName (when applicable), Kind, and the original SDK exception as InnerException.

Acceptance is ExporterTests.cs — it points a genuine AmazonCloudWatchLogsClient (dummy credentials, MaxErrorRetry=0) at a loopback fake speaking the documented x-amz-json-1.1 protocol. Run: dotnet test — until green. ExporterTests.cs, CloudWatchTokenExport.csproj, .gitignore and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: CloudWatch Logs API Logs_20140328 via AWS SDK for .NET V4 (AWSSDK.CloudWatchLogs 4.0.101.2)

official_sources.title: DescribeLogStreams - Amazon CloudWatch Logs
official_sources.used_for: limit 1-50 (default 50), orderBy LogStreamName vs LastEventTime constraints, opaque nextToken that expires after 24 hours and is absent on the last page, the 25 transactions-per-second throttle, and logStream response fields

official_sources.title: GetLogEvents - Amazon CloudWatch Logs
official_sources.used_for: nextForwardToken/nextBackwardToken are never null and repeat the passed token at the end of the stream (the only documented termination signal - partially full or empty pages do not mean done), startFromHead must be true when paging with a forward token, event fields timestamp/message/ingestionTime in epoch milliseconds, and the errors InvalidParameterException/ResourceNotFoundException/ServiceUnavailableException

official_sources.title: PutLogEvents - Amazon CloudWatch Logs
official_sources.used_for: the documented correction that sequence tokens are now ignored and InvalidSequenceTokenException/DataAlreadyAcceptedException are never returned anymore - the catalog's 'invalid-sequence' requirement is therefore pinned as a terminal legacy error family, distinct from throttling, rather than a live retry case

official_sources.title: IAmazonCloudWatchLogs Interface | AWS SDK for .NET V4
official_sources.used_for: the official injectable client interface (Amazon.CloudWatchLogs.IAmazonCloudWatchLogs in AWSSDK.CloudWatchLogs) exposing DescribeLogStreamsAsync/GetLogEventsAsync with CancellationToken parameters

research.language_rationale: AWSSDK.CloudWatchLogs (AWS SDK for .NET V4) is AWS's current official .NET SDK for CloudWatch Logs; the exporter depends on the SDK's IAmazonCloudWatchLogs interface, and the tests point a genuine AmazonCloudWatchLogsClient at a loopback HTTP fake speaking the documented x-amz-json-1.1 wire protocol with dummy credentials.
